title: About
text:
Welcome! I am a climate researcher and R enthusiast. See my CV for full details.

Short Bio
Marc Lemus-Canovas is a Postdoctoral Researcher at Andorra Research + Innovation (Andorra). His research focuses on climate change and climate variability in the Pyrenees, with particular emphasis on high-resolution climate projections for this mountain region. He holds a PhD in Geography (Climatology) from the University of Barcelona, where he studied extreme precipitation events, synoptic climatology, and climate teleconnections across the Iberian Peninsula and the Pyrenees.


Research Interests

Climate variability and change in mountain regions
Extreme precipitation events and compound events
Teleconnection patterns (WeMO, NAO, AMO…)
Synoptic climatology and circulation weather types
Urban climatology (Barcelona Metropolitan Area)
High-resolution climate projections for the Pyrenees
R programming and open-source tools for climate analysis



The synoptReg R Package
As part of his doctoral work, Marc developed synoptReg, an R package for computing synoptic climate classifications and performing spatial regionalization of environmental data. The package is available on CRAN and GitHub.


R Programming & Data Science
R is central to both Marc’s research and his passion for open and reproducible science. He is enrolled in a BSc in Applied Data Science at the Open University of Catalonia (UOC), deepening his expertise in data analysis, machine learning, and statistical modelling.
This website serves as a place to share R tutorials and tips — mainly focused on climate data analysis, but also covering general data science workflows.


Contact
Feel free to reach out via email or through any of the social links at the bottom of this page.
Last updated on April 21, 2026

title: Marc Lemus-Canovas
text:
About
I am a Beatriu de Pinós Postdoctoral Fellow at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (Earth Sciences Department). My research focuses on understanding the link between atmospheric dynamics and extreme meteorological events — compound heat and drought events, extreme precipitation, and their attribution to climate change — with a particular emphasis on mountain areas (Pyrenees, Alps).
I hold a PhD in Geography from the University of Barcelona (2022), where I was awarded the Extraordinary Doctorate Prize. I have previously held postdoctoral positions at EURAC Research (Italy), Andorra Research + Innovation, and the University of Santiago de Compostela.
I am the principal investigator of HIPEAtMount (€172,750, Province of Bolzano), investigating the attribution of extreme precipitation events in the Alps, in collaboration with ETH Zürich. I am also coordinating an international network (KNMI, IPSL, SLF, Oxford) to develop an R/Python package for extreme weather attribution using flow analogues.
H-index: 14 (Scopus) · Citations: 500+ (Scopus), 677 (Google Scholar) · 29 publications (13 as first author)

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
It is possible to read a NetCDF file in a Tidyverse way through the metR package created by Elio Campitelli. This library also contains many useful functions for visualizing and analyzing weather and climate data.
In this tutorial, we will work with a daily grid of 5×5 km of the maximum temperature of the Balearic Islands (Spain) from the STEAD dataset (Serrano-Notivoli 2019).

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(metR)
library(trend)
library(pals) # color palettes

We use GlanceNetCDF() to inspect the file and ReadNetCDF() to read it into a data.frame:

file <- "tmax_bal.nc"

# Inspect the NetCDF structure
variable <- GlanceNetCDF(file = file)
variable

# Read the NetCDF as a data.frame
tx <- ReadNetCDF(
  file = file,
  vars = c(var = names(variable$vars[1])),
  out = "data.frame"
)

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
It is possible to read a NetCDF file in a Tidyverse way through the metR package created by Elio Campitelli. This library also contains many useful functions for visualizing and analyzing weather and climate data.
In this tutorial, we will work with a daily grid of 5×5 km of the maximum temperature of the Balearic Islands (Spain) from the STEAD dataset (Serrano-Notivoli 2019).

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(metR)
library(trend)
library(pals) # color palettes

We use GlanceNetCDF() to inspect the file and ReadNetCDF() to read it into a data.frame:

file <- "tmax_bal.nc"

# Inspect the NetCDF structure
variable <- GlanceNetCDF(file = file)
variable

# Read the NetCDF as a data.frame
tx <- ReadNetCDF(
  file = file,
  vars = c(var = names(variable$vars[1])),
  out = "data.frame"
)

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Data management: annual aggregation
After filtering NA values, we aggregate daily data to annual means per grid cell:

tx <- tx %>%
  filter(!is.na(var)) %>%
  as_tibble()

tx_annual <- tx %>%
  mutate(Time = as_date(Time)) %>%
  mutate(year = year(Time)) %>%
  group_by(year, lon, lat) %>%
  summarise(annual_tx = mean(var)) %>%
  ungroup()

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Trend computation and statistical significance
We apply the Sen’s slope (magnitude of trend per decade) and the Mann-Kendall test (statistical significance) at each grid cell:

tx_trend <- tx_annual %>%
  group_by(lon, lat) %>%
  summarise(
    slope = sens.slope(annual_tx)$estimates * 10,  # per decade
    sign  = mk.test(annual_tx)$p.value
  )

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Visualization

ggplot() +
  geom_tile(data = tx_trend, aes(x = lon, y = lat, fill = slope)) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = rev(pals::linearlhot(100)),
    name = "ºC/10y",
    limits = c(0.1, 0.45)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = filter(tx_trend, sign < 0.01),
    aes(x = lon, y = lat, color = "Sig. trend (p < 0.01)"),
    size = 0.4, show.legend = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black"), name = "") +
  coord_fixed(1.3) +
  xlab("Longitude") + ylab("Latitude") +
  labs(
    title = "Decadal trend of maximum temperature in the Balearic Islands",
    subtitle = "(1971–2014)",
    caption = "Database: STEAD (Serrano-Notivoli, 2019)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  guides(fill = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 9, barheight = 0.5, title.position = "right")) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

If you have any questions, feel free to leave a comment or reach out!
